common_name: Variegated Cast Iron Plant (Cream-Striped Deep-Shade Aspidistra Groundcover)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; the variegated form is somewhat slower-growing than the all-green species and slightly less tolerant of extreme neglect — provide consistent moisture and avoid direct sun which burns the cream variegation.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Variegated Cast Iron Plant is a stunning selection of the legendary Aspidistra elatior — the most shade-tolerant of all commonly grown ornamental plants — producing the same broad, strap-shaped, leathery evergreen leaves as the green species but with distinctive cream-white to ivory longitudinal stripes that create a luminous, light-reflecting effect in the deepest, darkest shade positions where few other plants survive. The cream stripes typically run the length of the leaf blade in widths varying from thin lines to broad bands, producing a gently variegated pattern that brightens dark garden areas while maintaining the bold, tropical foliage character that makes cast iron plant a cornerstone of Gulf South shade gardening. Aspidistra elatior earned its common name through extraordinary tolerance of neglect, drought, poor light, dry soil, and root competition from trees — but the variegated form is slightly more demanding than the green species and produces its best appearance with consistent moisture and protection from sun. In zone 9, Variegated Cast Iron Plant grows as a reliable perennial groundcover under live oaks, magnolias, and other dense-canopy trees where the combination of root competition, deep shade, and summer drought would eliminate most alternatives. The cream stripes are most vivid in the variegated form grown in good but indirect light — deep shade in poor soil dulls the variegation over time.

common_name: Black Mondo Grass (Deep Purple-Black Blade-Leaf Shade Groundcover)

planting.note: Plant clump divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Black Mondo Grass spreads slowly by short stolons — plant 8–10 inches apart for groundcover use, expecting 2–3 seasons to achieve full coverage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Black Mondo Grass is one of the most visually distinctive and useful low-growing plants for shade and partial-shade gardens — a slow-spreading groundcover producing narrow, grass-like leaves in an extraordinary deep purple-black to true black color, creating a stark, dramatic contrast that is unlike any other commonly available ornamental groundcover. The nearly-black color comes from extremely high anthocyanin concentrations in the leaf tissue — the most intense dark pigmentation achievable in a commonly grown groundcover plant. Small, pale lavender to white flowers in mid-summer are followed by dark blue-black berries in fall — the berries persist through winter. Black Mondo Grass is one of the most effective design plants in shaded gardens because its almost-black color creates negative space that makes surrounding lighter plants appear to glow by contrast — pale yellow, chartreuse, cream-variegated, or silver foliage plants adjacent to Black Mondo Grass gain visual intensity from the extreme contrast. In zone 9, it performs well in partial shade with consistent moisture; full sun is tolerated with adequate water but can cause bleaching of the dark color during peak summer heat. The slow spread means a groundcover planting requires patience — typically 2–3 growing seasons to achieve solid coverage at 10–12 inch spacing.

common_name: Gold Banded Liriope (Bold Gold-Edged Variegated Lilyturf for Partial Shade)

planting.note: Plant container plants or divisions in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Gold Banded maintains brighter, more vivid gold-yellow margins in partial shade — full sun fades the gold toward cream-yellow, while deep shade reduces the gold intensity further.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Gold Banded is a variegated liriope (lilyturf) cultivar producing the characteristic strap-shaped, grass-like leaves of Liriope muscari with an added golden-yellow to bright yellow longitudinal stripe along each leaf margin — a bolder, warmer variegation than the cream-white margins of the more common 'Variegata' liriope. The wide gold-yellow leaf margins (sometimes covering 30–40% of the leaf width) create a vibrant, luminous visual that brightens shaded positions with warm-color light rather than the cooler cream-white of standard variegated liriope. Gold Banded is fully compatible with all the same growing conditions as standard liriope: adaptable to a wide range of soil types (including clay), drought-tolerant once established, salt-spray tolerant for coastal positions, and reliable in the full range of Gulf South zone 8-10 conditions. The spike of small lavender-purple flowers in late summer (August–September) above the variegated foliage is attractive and provides late-season color. Black berries in fall are decorative and provide food for some bird species. The gold variegation makes Gold Banded an outstanding border accent for partial-shade edges, where the warm-gold margins create a bright linear effect that defines the border line against adjacent lawn or darker mulch areas.

common_name: Violette de Bordeaux Fig (Petite Negri Fig, Small Dark Purple Rich-Flavored Fig)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Violette de Bordeaux is among the best container figs available — the compact habit, heavy production on young wood, and excellent flavor make it ideal for large containers on patios where it can be moved for frost protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Violette de Bordeaux (also sold as 'Negronne' or 'Petite Negri') is a small to medium-sized fig tree producing small, round to pear-shaped fruits with dark purple-black to deep violet skin and strawberry-red to garnet-red interior flesh of exceptional sweetness and richness. The flavor is consistently ranked among the finest of all fig varieties for fresh eating — very sweet, complex, with a rich, jammy depth that distinguishes it from the milder white and green-fleshed figs. The compact growth habit (typically 6–10 feet with pruning, vs 15–20 feet for standard figs) and relatively small leaf size make Violette de Bordeaux the preferred choice for container fig culture — it produces heavily on young wood from small plants, fruits reliably in containers, and is manageable for moving indoors for frost protection in marginal climates. The smaller fruit size (typically 1–1.5 inches) means more fruits per harvest than large-fruited varieties, with each fruit producing one or two bites of exceptional flavor. In zone 9, Violette de Bordeaux is cold-hardy enough to survive most winters but benefits from a protected south-facing wall position or heavy mulching during freezes below 25°F. Self-fertile, like all common figs, requiring no pollinators.

common_name: Panache Tiger Fig (Ornamental Yellow-Green Striped Fig with Strawberry-Like Flavor)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Panache requires maximum sun to develop the best striped skin coloring and fruit sweetness — shaded fruits tend to be less sweet and less distinctively striped.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Panache (Tiger Fig, Strawberry Fig) is one of the most visually distinctive and ornamentally beautiful fig cultivars — producing medium-sized fruits with unmistakable yellow-to-green and darker-green vertical striping on the skin that resembles the pattern of a tiger or the appearance of a watermelon rind, creating an arresting visual display unlike any other commonly grown fig. The striped skin pattern is formed by differential ripening of the fruit skin, with the yellow portions ripening ahead of the green stripes and both shifting gradually toward gold-and-green as the fruit matures. The interior flesh is light strawberry-red to pale rose — the flesh color and an unusually sweet, almost strawberry-like flavor that gives 'Strawberry Fig' its other common name. The ornamental quality of Panache at all stages of fruit development makes it an outstanding specimen tree for edible landscape use — the striped fruits hanging in clusters from late summer through fall are genuinely decorative. Production is somewhat lower than some other cultivars — Panache prioritizes flavor quality over quantity. In zone 9, Panache is grown successfully as a standard landscape fig tree; it is somewhat less cold-hardy than varieties like Celeste and may need frost protection below 20°F.
